Well, welcome back and welcome to part two of sales mastery on leash the no BS system for closing sales like crazy In this segment We're going to be going over the first four steps of the no BS sales system including how to create an irresistible Offer so let's get started With right this Right away and we talked a lot about this in the last segment So I want to move right to the offer in the construction of the offer because truthfully most people get this wrong Without a good offer. It's really tough for the salesperson to actually make the sale So down what are some of the elements that need to be put into a good offer? the You want to give a salesperson the best as you say the best possible Proposition that they can put in front of somebody That you can afford and and ideally you If you have professionals you may want to give them some flexible pieces and parts that they can add on they can modify Etc. If you have Sort of lower level Sales people like in a retail store. You don't do that. You got to give them a locked in place Here's here's what it is Some of the elements are depending on what's being sold risk reversal So what is that? Well by some measure of guarantee Free trial apparent free trial you know some version of being able to get out Without being wounded if this turns out not to be as represented Okay, so you really don't need it With a jacket because you can try it on when you pick it up you can look at it in the mirror You can decide right automobile different story mattress different story. It always feels different 10 days after you got it all winter sleeping on it that it did laying on on the store and everybody knows it, right? So so you have to think about what level of risk reversal is needed for it to be irresistible To your point and then you try and get as close to that As you possibly can right a ratio of Cost to economic value if you are Selling money at a discount one way or another Or ratio to emotional value If you are not selling money at a disc discount explain what selling money at a discount is because I don't think a lot of people Know what that is well, so money it's really very simple it allows the The presentation of the offer to ultimately come down to If you knew they were real Um, you had no question about their validity and your bank had hundred dollar bills on sale today for 85 bucks How many would you want right and what lengths would you go to to round up as many 85s as possible? To go exchange for the hundred right that's money at a discount so uh to some people so net jets Uh fractional jet ownership Sells that as money at a discount a based on the value of the time Of the user because most people who are Buying one fourth of an airplane in order to fly private Their income is very high therefore their time is very valuable So a case can be made if I can get you back home today So you get to sleep in your own bed be refreshed and come to the office in the morning ready to play Versus you getting home at 11 o'clock at night if the flight's on time Not having good food and being irritated and what that has value right they also sell by emotional logic Most net jets advertising shows the the happy family The younger wife the kids so it's the second family with the Fairly affluent gentleman on the beach that he's able to be with them In south carolina and be at a board meeting Two hours later because he's a net jets customer so The ratios between value and cost are part of the offer the actual description of the stuff The you get this and you get this and you get this often great premiums Can Help or drive a sale Sometimes they're tried to tie to risk reversal Which is even if you don't like Even if you don't like x and return it for a refund you get to keep The bonus the premium It's a myth that doesn't work with affluence There's a There's a 27 million dollar house for sale right now in reno And the premium with purchase is you get the guys Bentley and his rolls Royce that are in the garage So And it's right there. I mean they're not they're not making any secret of it Um So all of those things go into the offer then the structure of the offer is what order do you Present do you lead with the risk reversal? Do you lead with the The price to value differential do you lead with the premium? Do you what order do you put these things in what emphasis do you put on and how do you figure that out? Well, you can you know Put about all of them have a monkey throw darts. I mean you can right? That's a gkic method if you are Don't tell all our secrets if you are Hey, at least we're using darts If you we lease the monkey though, it comes in what's a quarter Yeah, and he's skinny because there's no budget for bananas Um Oh So So you can do that, right? Yeah, if you are very aware of your customer How they think how they operate etc and And you're fairly skilled at Marketing you can figure out What is the likely best? If you can you will get to it by testing right so Often you need to find a way to test that then migrates down to the salesperson Right, so if you so if right now if you go into sleep number bed store The the way the offer is presented to you Which leads with risk reversal? And they all do it one way They got there by split testing In their direct response Advertising and marketing and in their phone room And then they got to a best script Which then gets locked in at the sales level well, that's the beauty of The folks who are watching this who are gkic members because they know how to do this right with sales letters and because really it's salesmanship and print That's what a sales letter is and so it's going to be basically the same structure if you get it to work in the sales letter I mean for most cases you can follow that same structure with one-on-one salesperson as well In many cases if you could get away with it, right? You'd be better off having the salesperson sit there and read them the sales letter Then you would doing anything else right so yeah it migrates so Here's the thing that well we've heard many times nick and I want you to address this because it's a problem The offer this offer is so good that I don't really have to sell it People will just know that they want it. Well, it couldn't be further from the truth. I mean well, we've heard it We think we hear it right and I think we think that as business owners or has the person crafting the Crafting the offer, but it really when you're talking about face-to-face selling belly button to belly button Or if you're doing it over the phone in the phone room There needs to be some level of presenting the offer walking them through it. You're also testing I think Dan you're absolutely right of you're listening to what gets people excited what gets people You know, that's really not a benefit for me, but you know the think that they offer so good We don't have to tell anybody about it. They're just going to find their way to us I think is the quickest way to find their way Yeah, not even find their way to us, but I think the offer is so good that I or let's take it to the extreme It's a free offer. I'm offering a free consultation. I'm offering a free book. I'm offering. I don't have to sell it Dan, how would you address that? So So the departed Joan Rivers said Even the ugliest girl on the planet With an opportunity to sleep with Brad Pitt would still appreciate being asked So People Appreciate being asked right to buy Right, so first of all to his point It usually has to be explained For it to be understood as such a great offer Secondly, people like to be asked to buy They don't really like Ergon Selling They don't like their purchase being taken for granted They don't like A sense of entitlement In a sales person So they like to be asked third Often when you really create a spectacular offer It will engender some skepticism Right, which is one of the ways you know, it's really great Is if people are questioning, how can you do that or why would you do that or what can't I see? That must be wrong with this right which now requires that has to be addressed and dealt with So very rarely Is there such a A spectacular offer that it will Stand alone Right, so you have to sell you have to sell they want to be romance. They think you know we talk about it You know that people enjoy to buy that's a key word there by the way Romance and selling I mean it really is they want to be romance. They want to be loved They want to appreciation shown both before during and after the sale and I think it has to happen And I think it's part of the process and and you know the pros realize that and the pros So if you took the club, so if you took bob stupak's irresistible offer Which I've shown a number of times. It's in the archives collection, by the way Um, so his irresistible offer for many years the control For vegas world, which is now the stratosphere That brought in all the money to build vegas world from one floor to what it is um was Uh, and I may now be a tick off, but I'll be close to correct Um three nights two days um Deluxe room and the vip tower, which was the only tower Um, um all the booze you can drink whether you're gambling or in the lounge So there was no tabs in the lounge you drink for free um a few meals uh some premiums so you got Some fuzzy dice and stuff when you got there and Uh $600 to gamble with for 297 bucks So Everybody could do that math right now the 600 was match play, but even with match play it was 300 right So everybody could do that math so the The person of the school of if the offer is irresistible enough. We don't need any salesmanship for it You could buy a full page ad in a travel magazine A casino magazine and just state it the way I just stated it and people would buy it Not of course the case In stewpac's case if you find the ad you'll find it full of copy Not just about the offer but romancing the idea Of the experience you're going to have at vegas world and you're at the coolest place and you know all that If you've got a direct mail piece you've got an eight page sales letter and a brochure and And you've got some flattery Some positioning about having a high roller experience and vegas discriminates really against the ordinary working folk who i like So a whole lot of salesmanship went into This offer that on its face You should only have to state it and people should throw money at you And so if that's not the case with that right i defy you'd build one That is that has better economics Gotcha, gotcha. All right, let's go to what seems to be you would think it would be a fundamental But man every time I I do this in a workshop people still get this one wrong the difference between features and benefits Let's go as fundamental as we can get here What's the difference between a feature and a benefit and What then we're going to a second question about this? I like to get this wiped off the map once and for all this problem because it's amazing that people still don't get this Right And it doesn't matter Where the salesperson is or what they're selling they always struggle with this And I think in its simplest form a feature is an attribute. It's a spec. It is Something it's tangible if you could use that word Where a benefit is lifts them up. It's the end result if you think about pain and pleasure You know that we move them towards the benefit of of of the product or remove them away from the pain of not having the product And I think people get confused because it's easy for us to think about features and it's it makes it it's quick Right and it goes back to laziness and selling where they won't use scripts and they won't do the homework And so we're just going to go in and we're going to throw the marbles We're going to throw all the features on the table to them and if you're a car salesman We're going to talk about how fast the engine is and zero to sixteen all that has created the benefits of having the car And we haven't done the work on the upfront We haven't done the diagnostic work to find out what benefits they're looking for What hot button benefits are they looking for so we could apply those in our presentation And by the way, there's a big disconnect often Um, you took this out of the context of offer now you're right Yeah, you're not talking about features and benefits of the offer. You're just talking about features and benefits in general Right, so you will constantly see because salespeople are not Good at it at this Or not managed so that they are good at it You'll see a big disconnect between what's going on in the advertising and what's going on on the sales floor So the advertising people will actually get this right At the front end of this whole process So you'll see a car commercial or a series of car commercials that is all about Being the parent of a teenager that That teenager is just getting their driver's license and You having picked the car because It thinks for itself and it protects this kid and you can actually hand the keys to the kid and go in the house and watch the game and not You know be by the window Waiting for the sound of the crash So they'll get this right But then to his point if you now get to the car dealership And you get with the car salesman. There's a complete disconnect that has happened Along the way and the car salesman will not pick up on that conversation He will not refer to that conversation. He won't be emotionalizing. He'll be talking about How that it's the airbags? Now he's in the same place But he's defaulted down to features Right the things got six airbags Honda's Duma flage has only got four and ours deploy With Six pounds less impact and so he's going to have an airbag conversation and The customer isn't even sophisticated enough to appreciate the conversation. I mean Who knows right but the disconnect occurs So often the advertising people will get it right They will be in the benefit territory And it will disintegrate down to feature territory at the selling end You know one of my First people that taught me about selling when I was in radio. She says whenever you Name a feature because sometimes you do have to talk about the feature by the way I think a lot of people say well you never talk I've read that in sales books don't talk about features at all Well, which is wrong because it goes back to the emotional logic that we talked about in the last segment What she said was whenever you say a feature though add which means to you And I just thought or you don't have to verbally say it every single time But if you think about it, so you say what the feature is so this has six airbags, which means to you Then you automatically get to the benefit. I also think that you know We don't want to downplay features because in some areas features are important the specs You know if you're selling fda regulated or if you're selling in healthcare you're selling In computers, you know There's going to be somewhere along the line where there there needs to be a matchup of the features to make sure that it's Compatible with what the objectives of the organization are whether that's a computer program that has to talk to another computer program Or if it has to be regulated by the fda so the specs in some sense matters But the pros will focus on the features, you know the The outcome of the surgery not the thing of a jigger bobs that caused the surgery to happen, you know within the or Yeah, see even in those cases There is so you will have b2b people Raise this b2b is different than consumer All right, all right Even in the environments that he just discussed The tendency Is now to heavily weight fact statistics comply, but There's almost an assumption On the part of the buyer in those environments that you are meeting the minimum ante Nobody really from the saw will use your fda example So nobody is really going to be sitting across from the buyer of a nutritional supplement company Selling their labeling and packaging services Who does not The buyer is going to assume By the very fact that they're there and that they do labeling and packaging for nutritional supplements That they meet the minimum standards of the fda, right? Um, i have a client i'm working with right now the state of oklahoma to get business system moved to oklahoma so i'm Over here with the romance of why you want to be In oklahoma the salespeople are over with the six percent tax rebate for the first right Every state does that Even horrible business states do that Right, that's like the minimum ante to get at the game table. We can assume They know they can work you for a tax rebate Right, if he if he's a site selection consultant who is representing a company and looking at five states He knows he can he can get me for a tax rebate That's really not a question right, but It's really the other stuff That will make somebody Choose one or the other want to be there and so the tendency is to put way too much weight On facts features statistics Compliance All of that and not enough on the intangible emotional What does this mean To own this yeah, what's my outcome going to be what's my transformation going to be All of that and by the way, I know we're going over a lot of material here. What's really important is that you do read your transcript That's why we had this transcribe for you because so Dan went over all the elements of the offer unless you were taking crazy fast notes You're going to miss it, but just go back to your transcript and everything will be in there. Okay, so we talked about benefits And I read something interesting it was in the book spin selling great book on selling and What what the author said I think is Neil Neil rockman rockman rockman What he said is it's stuck with me. It's not a benefit unless your prospect Says it's a benefit unless they agree that that's a benefit to them Do you guys agree with that? mostly, okay, so That's what I call personal relevance. Okay personal And again in b2b selling a very common mistake is dealing with corporate relevance and never getting to personal relevance right Hope everybody got that because that was dad. That's key. He repeated again. I mean honestly, I want to make sure people got this This is important. There's a tendency to talk about corporate relevance Relevance of benefits to the entity and never getting to personal relevance for the human being or beings that are actually going to Drive and sign off on this purchase and just as Tip O'Neill famously said all politics is local All buying If not personal at least has a personal element in it It arguably shouldn't in b2b if you're a professional purchasing agent for xyz company You should be making all those decisions purely on corporate relevance None on personal relevance, but you won't because you're not a robot And hardly anybody has that kind of self-control So your career interests Your personal and reputational risk Of switching vendors. So a whole lot of personal stuff comes into this game And so to that extent I would agree with them where I would Put some caution on the table Is That often somebody doesn't know That a benefit Is personally relevant unless the advertiser marketer Copywriter and or seller Makes it Personally relevant beautiful beautiful. Yeah, and so the idea that Agreement in advance In a sense that we let the buyer Define the agenda Is I think a mistake. Oh, that's a that's a really great distinction So because so what you're basically saying is that the buyer is not educated enough in what you're selling possibly That they don't know that why this why the airbag using a horrible example Is is a major benefit or why the six airbags as opposed to the four airbags is a major benefit You you got to tell them about that. So that well that goes right This is basically this a similar question, which is the hot button close So first of all describe what the hot button closed and then that's then let's talk about well You know, is that true? Should we just be using the hot button close? Well, you know, um goes back to if you're doing a diagnostic interview or you're going through We want the hot buttons to really focus on all the senses So the the better you could do is by touching all the different senses because you don't know what the buyers You know relevancy is are they Are they a visual learner? Are they you know, are they a touch? You know, so if you have a product that you get in their hands and touch and those type of things So when you're going through all the benefits, you're looking for their responses to find out really what their hot buttons are If you're reading them, you know, if they're leaning forward if they're asking more probing questions about certain benefits I you know, that's going to be a hot button for them So you really want to focus on on the hot buttons that they really kind of raised their hand over the course of time You know, should you use all the benefits? I I really want to set myself up for success And any sale that I'm going into so I'm going to use as many benefits as I can Without steam rolling over the person to really make the case for the purchase dad well, so here's what that looks like and it's Perfectly okay unless you can do better So what that looks like is an inverted pyramid And we're starting at the top So we are presenting 12 benefits Okay, and based on feedback We are determining that Six of them Are far more important to you than the other six As we come down to pyramid now we throw six on pretty much aside and we talk more about the six we kept ultimately We're determining that two of those six are quote quote hot buttons for you By the time we get down to the to the bottom to the peak The narrowest part of the pyramid and we're about to close the only thing we're talking about is the two Okay, that's That's the process he just described Okay What's better If and when It can be done and often marketing And preparation Can help us Is if we can be further along in that pyramid Start narrow or based on pre-information Right, so for example, for example, okay, there's very basic pre-information By age bracket By gender By married or single By income that often allows us to make some of these calculations Without having To go through Right that process right Sometimes we can get The prospect to tell us those things In advance If they come to a group presentation Out of which they book a one-to-one appointment if they fill out a questionnaire Before that at the thing before they come to the we can Design the questionnaire to have them basically rank their hot buttons For us In advance, right? If they go to a website and they self select Right and there's four Choices they really now pick a Shorter narrow or pyramid that we are Going to sell in right If you were doing this as a sales presentation And you did a very simple self-select I i'm a salesperson who sells personally. I'm a sales manager who manages salespeople And they chose that You would then be able to start his process in each case at a different spot right focus on fewer hot buttons Still sifting and sorting if we were selling one-on-one But be more focused in the first place right if we prepare in one-on-one selling And we Do some research right so i'm going to come meet with you if i google you if i wikipedia If i facebook you if i amazon you some hot buttons may become Apparent right if you go call on somebody in their office And they have Fishing stuff everywhere We know first of all we know that the leisure time And recreation Is very important to this person So we probably know that that's a hot button if you if i come into your office and there's none of that All right, it's all business right right then i know There's that That hot button probably isn't there right right so if i'm going to test it at all I'm going to like test it as a little old by the way right and be off of it right so The process is valid we can be We can have different levels of sophistication about it depending on how much we prepare And how much information we gather and use before we get To the sales process. Yeah, that's the exact procedure I use when i was selling done for you doing done for you webinars and tele seminars and launches for people They'd have to qualify and then they had to fill out an application But the application really told me what their pain was what they were trying to achieve What was holding them back then when i got them on the telephone Well, I have their hot buttons That they just told me openly what they are which makes the selling process Uh makes the selling process that more valuable that much more valuable I think uh dan brought up a good point that we don't want to gloss over because a lot of people Won't do this a lot of salespeople won't do the but they'll say they can't do it They can't do it. They won't do the background information. So they won't won't do any level of research about the clients that they're in front of You know, there's so much information, you know google It is easy and you know, especially in the b2b world People are putting their lives out on in the public domain. So really doing that background You allow you to tease out hot buttons before you even get into the into the equation It allows you to have some level of of uh rapport building skills And you'll have the ability to do when you're in front of that person also Yeah, one of the things that Brings up a big thing because a lot of people I think would think well, I can't do that I can't send out the questionnaire ahead of time. I can't and that's Wrong thinking first of all because in most cases you probably can and doesn't that position you better if you want to go as a As a consultant, right? I mean before people get to you for your consulting day, which i'm going to come to in just a minute because it's important I don't think people think that you sell actually, you know, uh, but before they come to you They got to jump through all of these hoops to get to you And for the most part for the most part, right? Yeah once while there's a leaper who appears to be qualified, right? About 50 percent of the time I regret that right Because i'm now with a poorly prepared right person, right? And unlike many I don't use a formal questionnaire, right? People like j. Abraham for example do right, but yeah, there's a series of qualifying hoops That if they don't do anything else they tell me A who to stop before We go any further by behavior that is You know markedly incompatible with what i'm going to have to have in order to be effective With this person They also teach the person before they get to the sales event what to expect so there's a certain amount of Expectation management That goes into a diagnostic consultative Or sophisticated selling process Part of this is you have to decide in advance what he's going to have to know And accept In order to buy from you or do business with you So for me, that's extraordinarily high fees And limited access those are going to be the two deal breakers that at the end When it's actually time to say to prescribe now Well, what you need is these 18 things and I can do 16 of them for you and here's what that Calculates out to and how do you want to take care of the money? The two things that will be deal breakers, which will be everything else a waste of time Right getting to that point right for me the two deal breakers are going to be What do you mean? I can't pick up the phone and talk to you right right Huh, what do you mean? I have to wait two weeks for an appointment. What do you mean? You don't use email What do you write that's going to be a deal breaker? Right or the amount of money is going to be a deal breaker can't afford it or just can't mentally wrap their head around the idea That it can cost this much right or that you're going to take a piece of the revenue In addition to the fees so those two things are going to be the deal breakers for me So I want to get that out of the way Long before we get to Face to face even though i'm paid to do the selling I because it's a paid consultation day rather than a Comp consultation still i'm not interested in that money I'm interested in The big money right so I don't I don't even I don't want 19 grand for the day if there's no reasonable hope Right of us going further right so In almost everything I just I just finished the no BS marketing the affluent book and the case history We have in the back of the book is from a this made me think of it is from a eyewear company in the uk Their average sale is five thousand dollars And they have a completely diagnostic consultative Sales process they have probably the only optical store In the world with no eyeglasses visible when you come into the store so There are certain things people have to know that If they just sort of rush them to the sales point and surprise them with the idea of A five thousand dollar Tab for eyeglasses. They are not good at clothes very many sales. I don't care how good the salesperson is right So the lead-up has to take these things into consideration Absolutely, I was watching tv the other day in a show on a squire channel and it was uh This is after the view and ellen view and ellen they call me johnny and he was in Paris france and he was at a high end boutique and part of the high end boutique had waters and it was a wall of water He talked about the most commoditized thing on the planet water is it but they brought out a person to Diagnostically interview him about what is the right water for him to purchase and it was a series of questionnaires So it's almost like a salmane that is coming in asking about the wine But this is for water and he's like, you know, this is the wall was as big as the wall behind us And the questions were you know long and then they prescribed the correct water for him for that moment in time for that meal I mean you talk about the most commoditized thing on the planet. I can commoditize it more And it's automated. So it's not that sophisticated But dr. Scholes figured out Instead of selling you foot inserts off a rack at walgreens if you stand on a machine first and it takes a Diagnosis of your feet and it then tells you blue 19 is yours They can charge twice as much as they could before for the same product, right? So So, you know diagnostic selling can be applied at a lot of different levels and if you marry it to Getting the dealbreakers taken care of Through the process rather than having them be ugly Barriers that you're trying to drag somebody over At the very end and if and if you are going to use a sales force So You I or he may be good enough to sell that way Okay I am I can't speak for you two guys, but but I am okay, but but I am You know I can let it get all the way to a 12 foot high Barrier with barbed wire on the top and I can drag this out of a bench over the barrier Okay, I don't like doing it and I don't have to do it Hardly at all anymore But I'm that good you are not going to get a hundred people to work for you who are that good, right? It's unscalable, right? So you've got to have a sales process that works for The worst guy you got not just the best guy you got because you can't get enough best guys Yeah, absolutely One of the things that it seems to be I'm just glancing at some of the questions that that are coming up is My my business is a different kind of thing I So again, this is goes to marketing, you know, well, I can't do risk reversal in my business I because I'm this I can't Send out something in advance of the credit. I don't know why anyone could send out something in advance of the presentation I mean before before meeting with somebody well the way reason I can't is if the the leap from advertising marketing to selling Is so compressed right and they're afraid They're afraid to open it back up, right? That's Okay, got you got you but Let's let's address this because again, it's the same thing that's coming up again Which is my business is different. My my selling is different. How would you guys address that because it's not There's almost no circumstance that All of this Strategic not just tactical approach, right can't be applied to So the problem of the immediate sale Has been solved in all sorts of businesses Okay in the death business It has always existed Okay, so you can't Stretch two weeks and send somebody a shock and awe package and so forth when they have a dead father on the couch In the living room, right? So they move the entire process In advance of need right so pre-need planning At the bottom of this burial insurance at the top of this pre coffin purchase Costco's in the coffin business So people get all that done ahead of time. They clean it up in the garage or make a coffee table out of it or you know Is that I haven't seen anybody making the coffee. There's there's probably a youtube video, which I've not seen I've only had it described to me of a guy who has had his very elaborate coffin turned into a couch And his answer is when I dropped dead. I'll just follow him right close the lid and I'm good to go off to the races So and people say you can't do death human. Yes, you can. Yes, you can so So they moved the whole thing Away from need Excuse me Prepaid legal is a movement of The entire advertising marketing and sales process Back in front of need instead of that point of need so that they can do these sorts of things and effectively sell So hardly any business Prohibits it it's people's own attitudes about it. That's all it is In some cases It's excuse making Right, that's really what it is is I want to disqualify myself from all this so I don't have to deal with it I'd rather have as I would say the world is full of people who would rather have a great excuse than a difficult opportunity And and so some of it is that Right, some of it is just You know being very homage standing in the center of a very small circle looking around and gee nobody else does it that way Therefore it can't be done that way But there's no business this can't be done. Yeah, the other thing is we didn't promise here a 101 mark 101 course on selling we wanted something a little bit more sophisticated and different than quite honestly a lot of the books that are still teaching stuff from the 1950s, which Some of it which is fine Some of which is Exactly very primitive. So let's go. We've got we've got a bunch of questions here. Okay. Well, here we go Boom, let's ask you. There's a risk reversal question. Let's just answer it as an example here. All right So we don't have the person's name because no, no, okay Okay, so how do how do I apply risk reversal to a consultant business? Or any other kind of business where you can really you really can't guarantee the results Well, in many cases you can't guarantee results if you choose to Of course you can't Unless of course you can't produce results, right, but So Jerry jones for years one of our members In selling direct mail programs to dentists has paid for the printing and the postage of the first campaign Triggering a 12 month contract providing certain results are achieved with that campaign because he is so sure Of what he can actually produce Okay You can often Guarantee some front portion Of what it is. So for many years, I don't do it anymore. I don't have to And it was all creative math anyway, but for many years I sold consulting the initial day three three things are going to happen at the end of the day one of three things One is you will have had a great day You're going to leave with all sorts of value. You'll be happy and that's as far as it's going to go, which is fine to There's going to be other stuff you're going to want me to do for you that will become evident During the course of the day and we will agree on that project work and you'll be happy and I'll be very happy The third thing that could happen is you have a horrible day. You think you've got no value You definitely don't want to do anything else, which is fine Anytime during the day or at the end of the day, I'll hand you check back to you And uh, we'll hug and you can go home. Okay. Now. I no longer do the third part of that Okay, and no guarantees were attached to the project Agreed to other than the meeting of deadlines and the delivery of the work But in all sorts of industries Guarantee barriers have been broken and get broken So enterprise right now is aggressively advertised selling cars not renting cars and you have a 30 day Bring it back get all your money back guarantee on the car So buy it take it home drive it for 30 days to pre-owned car if you don't want to keep it Bring it back. We give you all your money back. Not we give you another car not wait. No, no, no. Here's your money Right. So everybody in the car business Would have told you right up to this day That you can't make that math work. It's a horrible thing to do people will take advantage They will have 512 reasons why you can't do it And within another six months, you'll see somebody else doing it And probably within two years it'll it'll be the accepted norm in the industry Okay, because you'll be at such a competitive disadvantage Not doing it that it'll become mandatory. Iacocca changed the whole automobile warranty Industry the way and he was the only one and then everybody Caught up today since they're making such crappy cars. That's a real problem. I mean well yesterday forward We called 700,000 Toyota we called 800,000 in the same day. The question was about the person wanted you to talk a little bit more about romancing Sure, because you mentioned that earlier as far as the presentation you've got a during the presentation You need to romance your product and your the benefits and on that talk. Can you give an example? Of course, um, I think one of the things you know people love to buy right and they love the Ship of the buy and let's use do I mean they use one of the most commoditized Businesses out there in toilet paper. Well, and I befriended a an older owner of a toilet paper There's a theme going on here with this but he you know, it's it's about as commoditized as they come It's about as unsexy as they come But his position in selling was I'm going to know everything I possibly can about you has my prospect has my customer You're never going to have to worry about your inventory I'm going to come in or send people in on a weekly basis and do the inventory. We're going to have its spot order Halloween There are five kids you're going to have five of the largest happy apples you're ever going to see he remembered every Christmas every holiday And so he romanced the sale and then romanced the post sale over the course of time and it was about his Unsexy as a business as they come so I'm trying to use a very sexy business. By the way, so there's a business selling to a retailer A product that is good at command shelf space Most people would argue that the retailer is going to buy the product that has the highest margin Or has the lowest cost Or the biggest brand name that is doing the most advertising and there is no way to compete successfully For shelf space with those factors in b2b.
